\text{Step 1: Dive in with the Law of Sines to find the first angle } B_1.

\frac{\sin(B)}{b} = \frac{\sin(A)}{a}

\sin(B) = \frac{8.0\sin(34^\circ)}{11.2} \approx 0.3994

B_1 = \sin^-1(0.3994) \approx 23.5^\circ

\text{Step 2: Check if a second triangle is possible.}

\text{The theoretical second angle is } B_2 = 180^\circ - 23.5^\circ = 156.5^\circ.

\text{Check if it fits with the given angle } A: 34^\circ + 156.5^\circ = 190.5^\circ.

\text{Since } 190.5^\circ \ge 180^\circ \text{, the triangle overflows! There is exactly ONE valid triangle.}

\text{Step 3: Find the missing angle } C.

\angle C = 180^\circ - \angle A - B_1

\angle C \approx 180^\circ - 34^\circ - 23.5^\circ = 122.5^\circ

\text{Step 4: Use the Law of Sines to find side } c.

\frac{c}{\sin(C)} = \frac{a}{\sin(A)}

c = \frac{11.2\sin(122.5^\circ)}{\sin(34^\circ)} \approx 16.90

\sin(B) = \frac{8.5\sin(45^\circ)}{10.6} \approx 0.5670

B_1 = \sin^-1(0.5670) \approx 34.5^\circ

\text{Step 2: Check if a second triangle is possible.}

\text{The theoretical second angle is } B_2 = 180^\circ - 34.5^\circ = 145.5^\circ.

\text{Check if it fits with the given angle } A: 45^\circ + 145.5^\circ = 190.5^\circ.

\text{Since } 190.5^\circ \ge 180^\circ \text{, the triangle overflows! There is exactly ONE valid triangle.}

\text{Step 3: Find the missing angle } C.

\angle C = 180^\circ - \angle A - B_1

\angle C \approx 180^\circ - 45^\circ - 34.5^\circ = 100.5^\circ

\text{Step 4: Use the Law of Sines to find side } c.

\frac{c}{\sin(C)} = \frac{a}{\sin(A)}

c = \frac{10.6\sin(100.5^\circ)}{\sin(45^\circ)} \approx 14.74
